original_hypothesis.title: No ETH Refund on Overpayment in Mint Functions
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: mint() and burnOrSwapExternalToMint() use require(msg.value >= getPrice(col) * _numberOfTokens) but never refund the excess. For salesOption 2 (descending price), the price decreases over time, so users who calculate the price at submission may overpay significantly if their transaction executes in a later block. The excess ETH is added to collectionTotalAmount and distributed to artists/team, not refunded.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The >= comparison accepts any overpayment. Descending price auctions (salesOption 2) are specifically designed for price discovery, but users have no way to receive refunds on price differences. This systematically extracts excess value from minters.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(msg.value >= (getPrice(col) * _numberOfTokens), 'Wrong ETH'); followed by collectionTotalAmount[col] = collectionTotalAmount[col] + msg.value; No refund of msg.value - (getPrice * tokens).

preconditions: Collection uses salesOption 2 (descending price) for maximum impact
User sends more ETH than current price requires

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Descending price: user estimates price at time T, tx executes at T+N where price is lower. Difference is lost.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Protocol captures excess ETH. Individual user losses proportional to price decay rate * confirmation delay.

economic_questions: What is the maximum overpayment exposure for salesOption 2?
How much ETH could be lost per user in typical network conditions?
Is this intentional design or a code oversight?
validation_requirements: Calculate price difference across typical block confirmation delays
Test mint with excess ETH and verify no refund occurs
Compare collectionTotalAmount growth with expected mint prices

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Protocol captures excess; per-user loss scales with price volatility

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Excess ETH above actual price at execution time

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Rate of price descent in salesOption 2; typical block delay
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare msg.value capture vs getPrice at execution

validation_result.impact_summary: Users who send more ETH than the current mint price lose the excess permanently. The overpayment is added to collectionTotalAmount and distributed to artists/team rather than refunded to the minter. This is especially impactful for salesOption 2 (descending price) where price can drop between transaction submission and execution.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The mint(), burnToMint(), and burnOrSwapExternalToMint() functions all use `>=` in their ETH value checks and then add the full msg.value (not the required price) to collectionTotalAmount. There is no refund of the difference between msg.value and the actual required price. For salesOption 2, the price decreases over time, so a user who estimates the price at transaction submission time may overpay significantly if the transaction executes in a later block when the price has dropped. The excess ETH becomes part of collectionTotalAmount and is distributed via payArtist(), effectively transferring value from minters to the artist/team recipients.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is accurate. Lines in mint() show `require(msg.value >= (getPrice(col) * _numberOfTokens), 'Wrong ETH')` followed by `collectionTotalAmount[col] = collectionTotalAmount[col] + msg.value`. No refund logic exists anywhere in the contract. The same pattern repeats in burnToMint() and burnOrSwapExternalToMint().

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The >= check allows any overpayment. The full msg.value (not getPrice(col) * _numberOfTokens) is added to collectionTotalAmount. No refund of excess ETH is performed.
Same pattern: >= check with full msg.value added to collectionTotalAmount, no refund of excess.
Same pattern: >= check with full msg.value added to collectionTotalAmount, no refund of excess.

salesOption 2 implements a descending price mechanism. The price changes based on block.timestamp, meaning the price at transaction submission can differ from the price at execution time. Combined with the >= check and no refund, users systematically overpay.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls mint() with msg.value based on estimated price at submission time
2. Transaction enters mempool and waits for inclusion
3. For salesOption 2, getPrice(col) may return a lower value at execution time than at submission
4. require(msg.value >= (getPrice(col) * _numberOfTokens)) passes because msg.value exceeds current lower price
5. Minting loop executes: gencore.mint() called for each token
6. collectionTotalAmount[col] += msg.value (full amount, not getPrice * tokens)
7. No refund of (msg.value - getPrice(col) * _numberOfTokens) is performed
8. Excess ETH is permanently captured in collectionTotalAmount
9. payArtist() later distributes the full collectionTotalAmount including overpayments
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The defect is in the accounting: collectionTotalAmount accumulates msg.value rather than the actual required payment. With salesOption 2 (descending price), the getPrice() function returns decreasing values over time. A user who submits a transaction when price is X ETH may have it execute when price is (X - delta) ETH. The full msg.value of X ETH is captured, and the delta is never refunded. This is a code defect regardless of salesOption because even with fixed pricing (salesOption 1), a user who accidentally sends more ETH than required loses the excess.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that configure salesOption. The defect exists regardless of configuration - it affects all sales options.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an 'attack' per se but a systematic code defect affecting all minters. Scenario: A collection uses salesOption 2 with collectionMintCost = 1 ETH, collectionEndMintCost = 0.1 ETH, rate = 0 (exponential decrease), timePeriod = 600 (10 min). User checks price at block N and sees 0.5 ETH. They submit tx with msg.value = 0.5 ETH. By the time the tx is mined (say 2-3 blocks later), getPrice() returns 0.48 ETH. The require passes (0.5 >= 0.48), but collectionTotalAmount gets 0.5 ETH, not 0.48 ETH. The 0.02 ETH difference is permanently captured. Over many minters and larger price movements, this accumulates to significant losses. Even without salesOption 2, any user who sends 1 wei more than required loses that wei permanently.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. For fixed-price sales (salesOption 1 or 3), impact is limited to accidental overpayment. For descending price sales (salesOption 2), the impact is systematic: every minter whose transaction is delayed by even one block during active price decay will overpay. The overpayment scales with the rate of price descent and network congestion. In high-traffic minting events with descending price auctions, cumulative losses across all minters could be significant.
recommended_mitigations: Refund excess ETH after minting: uint256 requiredAmount = getPrice(col) * _numberOfTokens; require(msg.value >= requiredAmount); ... collectionTotalAmount[col] += requiredAmount; if (msg.value > requiredAmount) { payable(msg.sender).call{value: msg.value - requiredAmount}(''); }
Alternatively, use strict equality: require(msg.value == getPrice(col) * _numberOfTokens) - though this is less user-friendly
Add a maxPrice parameter so users can specify the maximum price they're willing to pay, reverting if the current price exceeds it while still refunding the difference if the price is lower
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all contracts using fixturesDeployment.js. 2. Create a collection with salesOption 2 (descending price), e.g., mintCost=1 ether, endMintCost=0.1 ether, rate=0, timePeriod=60. 3. Set collection phases with allowlistStartTime = block.timestamp. 4. Advance time by some periods so price has decreased. 5. Call mint() with msg.value = initial high price (e.g., 1 ether) when current getPrice() returns less (e.g., 0.5 ether). 6. Verify that collectionTotalAmount equals 1 ether (not 0.5 ether). 7. Verify that the minter received no refund. 8. Call payArtist and verify the full 1 ether is distributed.
uncertainties: Whether this is an intentional design choice or a code oversight is unclear from the code alone
The practical magnitude of overpayment depends on network congestion and timePeriod configuration for salesOption 2
additional_testing_needed: Quantify typical overpayment amounts for realistic salesOption 2 configurations under various network conditions
Test whether frontends or documentation warn users about the >= behavior
Test edge case where getPrice returns 0 (if possible) - user could still send ETH and it would all be captured
